content.problemStatement:
You are given a **binary** string SSS of length NNN.

Your task is to find any permutation†^\dagger† PPP of [1,N][1, N][1,N] that satisfies the following conditions:


For each index iii (1≤i≤N1 \le i \le N1≤i≤N),

If Si=1S_i = 1Si​=1, there exists a contiguous subarray of PPP having length iii whose maximum element is iii.

More formally, there must exist a pair of integers (L,R)(L, R)(L,R) such that 1≤L≤R≤N1 \le L \le R \le N1≤L≤R≤N, (R−L+1)=i(R-L+1) = i(R−L+1)=i, and

max⁡(PL,PL+1,…,PR)=i\max(P_L, P_{L+1}, \ldots, P_R) = imax(PL​,PL+1​,…,PR​)=i.
If Si=0S_i = 0Si​=0, then for every contiguous subarray of PPP having length iii, its maximum element is not iii.

More formally, for every pair of integers (L,R)(L, R)(L,R) such that 1≤L≤R≤N1 \le L \le R \le N1≤L≤R≤N, (R−L+1)=i(R-L+1) = i(R−L+1)=i, it must hold that max⁡(PL,PL+1,…,PR)≠i\max(P_L, P_{L+1}, \ldots, P_R) \ne imax(PL​,PL+1​,…,PR​)=i.


If there are multiple possible permutations that satisfy the required conditions, you may find any one of them.

If there are no valid permutations, print the single integer −1-1−1 instead.

†^\dagger† A permutation of the integers [1,N][1, N][1,N] is an array of length NNN that contains every integer from 111 to NNN exactly once each.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the length of the string.
The second line contains a binary string SSS of length NNN.




Output Format
For each test case,



If no valid permutation exists, print the single integer −1-1−1 on a new line.
Otherwise, print NNN space-separated integers P1,…,PNP_1, \ldots, P_NP1​,…,PN​, denoting the permutation you found.

If there are multiple possible permutations that satisfy the required conditions, you may find any one of them.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: With P=[2,3,1]P = [2, 3, 1]P=[2,3,1], we have the following:

There is a subarray of length 111 whose maximum element is 111, namely the subarray [1][1][1].
There is no subarray of length 222 whose maximum element is 222.
The subarrays of length 222 are [2,3][2, 3][2,3] and [3,1][3, 1][3,1], with maximums 333 and 333, respectively.
There is a subarray of length 333 whose maximum element is 333, namely the subarray [2,3,1][2, 3, 1][2,3,1].

This satisfies the string S=101S = 101S=101.
Test case 222: It can be proved that no valid permutation exists for the string S=011S = 011S=011.
